The big thing I wanted to say is that, and this kind of, one of the things I said earlier is from media back to manual labor. I mean this really is a great direct sales product. And a lot of the classic direct sales products, the businesses have been dying because the products have been commoditized. Right, and so the vacuum cleaner business, it still exists, but a shadow of its former self. Right, there's only two companies left in the business who direct sell Interstate, the little green pig and Kirby. And they really only work in rural and small town areas and all that because you got Oric and all those guys on TV and you got stores and you got, you know, and so, and the Costco and on, on, on, on. So the fire alarm business, same way, the fire alarm business, a shadow of its former self. There's only two companies left in the industry, one of them's a client of mine, and, and it only works in certain areas and, you know, because everybody's got a smoke detector and everybody thinks that's enough and $8,000 worth of alarms is a hard sale. So the products in this business have sort of become problems. Now you do have, but you have Boomer stuff that's replaced it. So you have the bathtub with the walk in door business. You have Craftmatic beds who are still very big, big as they ever were. And that's like a $5, $6,000 pop. Right. There's a need for, because the, the, the sales system works and it works beautifully. There's a need for good product for that system. And there's a lot of good people out there to be had who are still struggling. They don't want to abandon the system. And they're in these industries where it's getting increasingly hard to sell and increasingly hard to get salespeople to sell because the products become so commoditized. So this is a big direct sales business. You're suggesting.
